nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in shallow tropical and subtropical seagrass meadows and other soft-bottom habitats, where it grazes on benthic algae and detritus.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with established algae growth
open sand or rubble flats for grazing
caves or overhangs for daytime shelter
loose shells and rubble it can use for self-decoration

diet.notes: Generally regarded as an effective grazing herbivore on hair algae, turf algae, film algae, and (per multiple aquarist reports) cyanobacteria sheets. Has been documented eating fish feces and uneaten food when present. Supplemental feeding with dried marine seaweed (nori) or pieces of macroalgae such as Ulva is commonly recommended once nuisance algae in the display has been consumed.

goodWith: established LPS and SPS corals secured to the rockwork
avoidWith: large wrasses known to attack invertebrates
any system dosed with copper medications

summary: A small-to-medium Caribbean sea urchin commonly used for hair-algae and cyanobacteria control in saltwater aquariums. Generally considered reef-safe and better-behaved than larger Echinothrix or pencil urchins.
careNotes: Caribbean species widely sold for algae and cyanobacteria control in marine aquariums; generally considered better-behaved (smaller body, shorter spines, less destructive movement) than larger Echinothrix or pencil urchins. Characteristically decorates itself with shell fragments, bits of rubble, and pieces of macroalgae carried on the aboral surface — a normal behavior, not a sign of stress. Spines are shorter and less needle-sharp than most tropical urchins, making handling somewhat less hazardous, though tube feet still grip strongly and the animal should be moved underwater rather than lifted. Generally regarded as sensitive to rapid changes in salinity, temperature, and pH; slow drip-acclimation over 60–90 minutes is widely recommended on arrival. Will not tolerate copper-based medications or sustained high nitrates — poor water quality is commonly reported to trigger spine loss, which usually precedes death. Should only be added to a mature, fully cycled system with established algae growth (most sources recommend a tank at least a few months old) because it grazes constantly and will starve in a sterile setup. Cross-source notes on tank size: LiveAquaria lists a 20 US gal (~76 L) minimum, while AlgaeBarn and SaltwaterAquarium.com recommend 30 US gal (~113 L); the larger figure was rounded up to 120 L per the project's tank-size convention (err on the side of caution; nice round values). Cross-source notes on adult size: LiveAquaria and AlgaeBarn cite a maximum of about 8 in (~20 cm) test diameter in the wild, while Tampa Bay Saltwater cites about 4 in (~10 cm) in captivity; the written range (8–15 cm) brackets the typical adult diameter most aquarists will see. Reef-safety is generally considered yes with the caveat that the urchin's bulldozer movement can topple unsecured coral frags and loose rockwork, and isolated reports of nipping at coralline encrustations or small zoanthids exist.
breedingNotes: Has been spawned in laboratory and aquaculture settings (broadcast spawner releasing gametes into the water column, with a planktonic echinopluteus larval stage that must be reared on phytoplankton through metamorphosis) but is generally not bred by home aquarists. Captive-bred juveniles are commercially available from a small number of producers (e.g., ORA, AlgaeBarn) and are the recommended source.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care guide; source for care level (easy), 20 US gal minimum, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, specific gravity 1.023-1.025, reef-safe rating, copper sensitivity, herbivore diet with nori supplementation, and family Toxopneustidae.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Designated secondary did not have a dedicated species page for Lytechinus variegatus (homepage only; BRS species/care-guide URL returned HTTP 404). Substituted with AlgaeBarn, Tampa Bay Saltwater, and SaltwaterAquarium.com (all on the saltwater echinoderm secondary list or referenced by it) for cross-verification.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn (Pincushion Urchins: The Algae Destroyers)
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Cross-confirmed Western Atlantic native range (seagrass meadows / soft-bottom), 30 US gal minimum per specimen, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, ~8 in max adult test diameter, hair/turf algae diet plus detritus, reef-safe with less-destructive movement than other urchins, and the Aristotle's-lantern grazing apparatus. Spines noted as not needle-sharp.

additional.name: Tampa Bay Saltwater (Pincushion Urchin - Lytechinus variegatus)
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Cross-confirmed reef-safe rating, nocturnal/grazing behavior, and the characteristic shell-and-rubble decoration habit. Cites a smaller ~4 in maximum captive size than LiveAquaria/AlgaeBarn — averaged into the written 8-15 cm adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquarium.com (Large Pincushion Sea Urchin)
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Cross-confirmed 72-78°F (22-25.5°C), pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, 30 US gal minimum, herbivore diet, and the shell-covering behavior. Notes mandatory 60-90 minute drip-acclimation due to extreme salinity sensitivity.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Lytechinus variegatus) / Toxopneustidae
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback. Confirmed family Toxopneustidae, order Camarodonta, class Echinoidea, common names 'green sea urchin' and 'variegated sea urchin', and Western Atlantic / Caribbean distribution.
